reviews.summary: This paper studies whether object-centric evidence (foreground-background agreement) can serve as a reliable update-safety signal for online test-time adaptation of vision-language models. The authors propose FOCUS, which gates StatA-style updates using agreement between full-image, foreground, and background views. Under a causal validation-threshold protocol, the paper finds mixed and overall negative results: FOCUS helps on Waterbirds but fails on CounterAnimal, and simpler background-only filtering often outperforms the full method.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed comprehensive online searches for related work. The key papers in this space include: (1) StatA (Zanella et al., CVPR 2025) - the baseline online adaptation method this paper builds on; (2) SegDebias (Wu & Cai, arXiv 2025) - uses segmentation for test-time debiasing of CLIP predictions; (3) ReTA/Liang et al. (ACM MM 2025) - introduces CER (Consistency-aware Entropy Reweighting) for cache-based TTA; (4) GS-Bias (Huang et al., ICML 2025) - learns global and spatial biases at the logit level. The FOCUS approach is distinct from these methods in that it specifically uses foreground/background view agreement to gate online adaptation updates rather than debias predictions or select cache entries. However, the core combination of object-centric evidence with online adaptation is incremental, and the paper's own results show the method is not consistently better than simpler alternatives. The negative result itself has some value, but the methodological contribution is limited given the extensive existing literature on object-centric debiasing and test-time adaptation for VLMs.
strengths: Scientific honesty: The paper openly reports negative/mixed results rather than cherry-picking favorable outcomes. This is commendable and adds value to the community.

Protocol correction: The revision properly distinguishes between causal (validation-derived) thresholds and non-causal exact-rate selection, correcting a methodological issue from the original version.

Experimental rigor: The paper includes appropriate baselines (entropy, max-probability, CER), ablations (agreement-only, foreground-only, background-only), and multiple seeds with statistical reporting.
Integrity: All experiment code is present, results.json matches the reported numbers, and the implementation is well-documented.
Latency accounting: The corrected end-to-end latency reporting (adding ~57ms for rollout and extra-view encoding) is more honest than cached gate-only numbers.
weaknesses: Negative results dominate: The central hypothesis is not supported - FOCUS fails on CounterAnimal and is outperformed by background-only ablation on both datasets. This limits the contribution.
Limited scope: Only two datasets (Waterbirds and CounterAnimal) are evaluated, both with the same CLIP ViT-B/16 backbone.
Mechanistic target not met: FOCUS achieves lower harmful-update AUPRC (0.0227 on Waterbirds) compared to simpler baselines like max-probability (0.1026) and CER (0.1935).
High computational cost: The ~57ms end-to-end latency per image is two orders of magnitude larger than cached gate-only costs, with limited downstream benefit.

Related work positioning: The paper could better distinguish itself from SegDebias (which also uses foreground/background decomposition) - the key difference (adaptation gating vs. prediction debiasing) is mentioned but not deeply analyzed.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a carefully conducted study with an honest negative result, which is valuable for the community. However, to strengthen the contribution for NeurIPS, I recommend: (1) Expand the analysis to explain WHY object-centric evidence fails as an update signal - the current paper reports the failure but doesn't provide deep insights into the mechanism. (2) Consider additional datasets beyond Waterbirds and CounterAnimal to establish generalizability. (3) The background-only ablation surprisingly outperforms the full FOCUS score - explore this finding more deeply as it may be an interesting result in itself. (4) Discuss more thoroughly why CER (consistency-based) outperforms FOCUS (object-centric) for harmful-update ranking - this could inform future method design. (5) The paper could be reframed as a 'lessons learned' or 'diagnostic study' that helps the community understand what signals do/don't work for update gating. The current framing as a method paper is weakened by the negative results, but as an empirical analysis it has merit.
questions_for_authors: Why does the background-only ablation outperform the full FOCUS score? Could filtering based on background suppression alone be a viable lightweight strategy?
The harmful-update AUPRC for FOCUS is much lower than CER and max-probability. What explains this mechanistic failure of object-centric evidence to rank harmful updates?
Did you investigate why CounterAnimal shows different patterns than Waterbirds? Is it related to the nature of the spurious correlations in each dataset?
Could FOCUS be combined with CER rather than used as a replacement? The signals seem complementary.
reviews.integrity_check: All verification checks passed: (1) The experiment source code (.py files) exists in exp/ directory and implements the described FOCUS method with proper StatA integration. (2) results.json numbers match the paper's Table 1 and Table 2 exactly (e.g., Waterbirds 50% rate: FOCUS 71.03% accuracy, entropy 62.04%, etc.). (3) Logs show actual training runs with epochs and losses. (4) The paper honestly reports negative results and claim_check in results.json shows 'overall_conclusion: Not supported or mixed under the corrected protocol'. No fabricated citations were found - all referenced papers (StatA, SegDebias, ReTA, GS-Bias, etc.) are real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues.

reviews.summary: The paper studies whether object-centric evidence from CLIP-derived foreground/background views can act as a safer update gate for online StatA adaptation of vision-language models. Under a validation-threshold causal protocol, FOCUS helps on Waterbirds but fails on CounterAnimal, and the paper ultimately presents this as a negative result: object-centric evidence is not a strong standalone write-safety signal. The manuscript is clear about this revised conclusion and includes ablations, sparse counterfactual harmful-update labels, and latency accounting.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks: searches for the exact title returned no obvious prior paper with the same framing, but searches around the core idea surfaced several very close neighboring lines of work. In particular, recent VLM TTA papers already study reliability-aware update selection and context/spatial debiasing, including Realistic Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models, A Lost Opportunity for Vision-Language Models, The Illusion of Progress? A Critical Look at Test-Time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models, GS-Bias: Global-Spatial Bias Learner for Single-Image Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models, SegDebias: Test-Time Bias Mitigation for ViT-Based CLIP via Segmentation, Fair Context Learning for Evidence-Balanced Test-Time Adaptation in Vision-Language Models, and Efficient Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models (TDA). I did not find an exact prior method that uses CLIP-derived object-centric foreground/background evidence specifically as a causal update gate for StatA, so the paper is not a duplicate. However, the contribution is still incremental: it is mainly a narrow recombination of existing ideas from spatial/context debiasing and reliability-gated TTA, and the paper's own results are negative.
strengths: The manuscript is unusually honest in reframing the work as a negative result rather than overselling the method.
The paper is clearly written and easy to follow; the distinction between causal thresholding and offline exact-rate analysis is explained well.
The study includes several useful controls, especially simple object-centric ablations showing that background-only filtering can outperform the full score.
The mechanistic question is concrete and testable: whether the gate identifies harmful updates better than lightweight baselines rather than merely improving headline accuracy.
The logs and stage artifacts suggest substantial experimentation was actually run rather than entirely fabricated.
weaknesses: The top-level results artifact does not match the paper's main table: several 50% rows in `results.json` are exact-rate values, while the manuscript's main claims use `exp/causal_threshold/results.json`. Under the stated review rules, a paper/results mismatch is a major integrity problem.

The currently provided executable pipeline does not appear to generate the primary `exp/causal_threshold/results.json` artifact used by the manuscript. In `exp/shared/focus_pipeline.py`, the main study uses exact-rate control for `entropy`, `maxprob`, and `focus`, so the code path for the primary causal-threshold table is not cleanly reproducible from the workspace.

Figure provenance is also somewhat inconsistent: the paper includes `figures/waterbirds_cumulative_accuracy.png`, while the report-stage artifact lists generation of `waterbirds_cumulative_accuracy_focus_vs_baselines.png`; the cited figure file predates the final report-stage outputs.

The experimental evidence is narrow: only two datasets are used, and the direct harmful-update labels are extremely sparse, especially on CounterAnimal (2 positives out of 192), which makes the main mechanistic metric unstable.
Novelty is limited because adjacent spatial/context-aware VLM TTA and debiasing papers already exist; the paper's main addition is the specific gating formulation and the negative finding.

Reference metadata is not fully clean. The cited works appear to be real, but at least some entries use outdated or incomplete venue information (for example TDA is cited as an arXiv preprint despite an available CVPR 2024 version).
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper would improve substantially if the artifact story were made internally consistent and fully reproducible. The first priority is to reconcile the manuscript tables, the top-level `results.json`, and the executable code path: either the main paper must be regenerated from the same canonical artifact exposed at the top level, or the top-level results file must be updated so every table number in the manuscript is directly recoverable from it. Relatedly, the code should include an explicit, auditable script or stage that produces the causal-threshold table now treated as primary; at present, the shared pipeline centers exact-rate evaluation for several methods, which undermines the claimed primary protocol. After that integrity issue is fixed, I would encourage the authors to sharpen the contribution as a carefully audited negative result, reduce novelty claims further, expand the discussion of why background-only filtering works so well, and either add one more benchmark or provide stronger uncertainty estimates around the sparse harmful-update labels. Cleaning the citation metadata and tightening figure provenance would also help.
questions_for_authors: Why does the top-level `results.json` expose exact-rate 50% rows for several methods when the manuscript's main table uses causal-threshold rows from `exp/causal_threshold/results.json`? Which file is intended to be the canonical raw-results artifact?
What exact code path generated `exp/causal_threshold/results.json`? I could not find a clean stage in the provided shared pipeline that emits this artifact as the primary main-study output.
Can you provide a reproducible script that regenerates the manuscript's main table directly from the workspace code and raw artifacts?
Why does the paper include `figures/waterbirds_cumulative_accuracy.png` while the report-stage artifact lists generation of `waterbirds_cumulative_accuracy_focus_vs_baselines.png` instead?
Would the conclusions remain unchanged if the harmful-update analysis were repeated with denser probes or a larger horizon, especially for CounterAnimal where positives are extremely rare?
reviews.integrity_check: The workspace contains real code, run logs, and stage artifacts, and many paper numbers do match the stage-specific artifacts (`exp/causal_threshold/results.json`, `exp/ablations/results.json`, and `exp/main_study/results.json`). However, the manuscript's main table does not match the top-level `results.json`, and the provided executable pipeline does not cleanly reproduce the primary causal-threshold table used in the paper. Under the stated review rules, that is a serious results-integrity problem and is the main reason for rejection.

reviews.summary: This paper studies whether object-centric evidence (foreground/background decomposition via CLIP attention rollout) can serve as an update gate for StatA-style online test-time adaptation of vision-language models. The paper tests the FOCUS gating method on Waterbirds and CounterAnimal under a carefully corrected causal threshold protocol and reports an honest negative result: FOCUS helps on Waterbirds but fails on CounterAnimal, does not achieve the best harmful-update ranking on either dataset, and is outperformed by a simpler background-only ablation.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Extensive online searches found no prior work proposing foreground/background evidence as a binary update gate for StatA-style online VLM adaptation. The closest related work is SEraser/InTTA (AAAI 2025), which uses SAM-based FG/BG decomposition for CLIP TTA but as a dual optimization loss, not an update gate. DeYO (ICLR 2024) uses object-destructive augmentation for sample selection in TTA but targets CNNs, not VLMs. SegDebias and GS-Bias use spatial/segmentation information for CLIP debiasing but not for gating online updates. The specific combination (FG/BG agreement as a StatA update gate) appears novel, but the novelty is incremental: it is a straightforward application of a well-known signal (foreground/background decomposition) to a specific adapter update rule, and the paper itself demonstrates it does not work. The paper also misses the SEraser/InTTA comparison entirely, which is the most directly related prior work.
strengths: Exemplary honesty: the paper clearly reports a negative result and does not oversell the method, explicitly stating the central hypothesis is not supported overall.

Careful protocol correction: the distinction between causal validation-derived thresholds (primary) and offline exact-rate ranking (secondary) is methodologically valuable and addresses a real issue in TTA evaluation.

Strong reproducibility: all hyperparameters, seeds, stream construction details, and result file provenance are explicitly documented. The code implements what the paper describes (verified: attention_rollout, foreground/background view construction, FOCUS score computation all match the paper's equations).

Transparent ablation design: testing agreement-only, foreground-only, background-only, no-background, soft-gate, and independent-mask variants provides thorough decomposition of the method's components.

All numbers in the paper's tables match the underlying results.json and exp/causal_threshold/results.json files exactly (verified: zero-shot CLIP 81.93%, plain StatA 72.49%, entropy 62.04%, CER 63.28%, FOCUS 71.03% on Waterbirds; FOCUS 62.30% on CounterAnimal; all std values match). All 19 bibliography entries verified as real publications.
weaknesses: Extremely limited evaluation scope: only two datasets (Waterbirds, CounterAnimal). For a paper studying whether object-centric evidence generalizes as an update-safety signal, testing on two context-bias benchmarks is insufficient. Standard TTA benchmarks (ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, VisDA, domain adaptation datasets) are entirely absent.

Harmful-update labels are too sparse to draw conclusions: 14 positives out of 192 on Waterbirds (7.3%) and only 2 out of 192 on CounterAnimal (1.04%). With 2 positive labels, the CounterAnimal AUPRC/AUROC numbers are essentially meaningless, yet the paper uses them to make comparative claims.

The paper misses the most directly related prior work: SEraser/InTTA (AAAI 2025) uses SAM-based foreground/background decomposition for CLIP test-time adaptation. This is the closest competitor and should have been discussed and compared.

The negative result provides limited mechanistic insight into why object-centric evidence fails. The paper observes that background-only outperforms the full score but does not explain the underlying reason, limiting the paper's value as a diagnostic negative result.
FOCUS is approximately 200x slower than baseline gates (~57ms vs ~0.3ms per image) for no net benefit. The cost-benefit analysis is so unfavorable that the practical contribution is negligible.

Entropy and max-probability gates produce identical downstream accuracy on Waterbirds (both 62.04% with identical std), suggesting they generate nearly identical accept/reject decisions, reducing the effective number of distinct baselines.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper has strong experimental integrity and commendable honesty, but its contribution is too narrow for a top venue. To strengthen the paper: (1) Expand to at least 3-4 additional benchmarks including standard TTA settings (ImageNet-C/R/Sketch, VisDA) to test whether the negative result generalizes or is specific to these two datasets. (2) Address the sparse harmful-update label problem by either increasing the probing density or using a different evaluation methodology that does not rely on only 2-14 positive examples. (3) Add a comparison with SEraser/InTTA and discuss why their FG/BG loss-shaping approach differs from your gating approach. (4) Provide deeper analysis of why background-only outperforms the full FOCUS score — is it because the agreement term is noisy, because foreground confidence is poorly calibrated, or because the score combination is suboptimal? (5) Consider whether the attention-rollout-derived masks are the right tool; the independent-mask sanity check (spectral saliency) shows large sensitivity to mask source, but only one alternative is tested. A SAM-based mask comparison would be informative and connect to SEraser.
questions_for_authors: Why was SEraser/InTTA (AAAI 2025), which uses SAM-based FG/BG decomposition for CLIP TTA, not discussed or compared?
With only 2 positive harmful-update labels on CounterAnimal, how confident are you in any of the CounterAnimal AUPRC/AUROC comparisons? What would the results look like with denser probing?
Can you provide intuition for why background-only gating outperforms the full FOCUS score? Does this suggest the foreground/agreement components are actively harmful to the gate decision?

Have you considered testing on standard TTA benchmarks (ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R) where context bias may be less extreme? The current datasets are specifically designed around spurious correlations, which may not be representative.
The entropy and maxprob gates give identical accuracy on Waterbirds — did you verify they produce the same accept/reject decisions, and if so, why report them as separate baselines?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is strong. All numbers in the paper's main table (Table 1) and ablation table (Table 2) were verified against exp/causal_threshold/results.json and exp/ablations/results.json respectively, with exact matches on means and standard deviations. Experiment source code exists in exp/shared/focus_pipeline.py and implements the described method (attention rollout over last 6 ViT blocks, foreground/background view construction, FOCUS score formula). The external StatA implementation is present. The consolidated results.json includes a claim_check section that honestly records 'pilot_pass: false' and 'overall_conclusion: Not supported or mixed under the corrected protocol'. All 7 spot-checked references (including future-dated arXiv papers from 2025-2026) were verified as real publications with correct metadata. No evidence of fabrication or result manipulation.

(2) Consider additional datasets beyond Waterbirds and CounterAnimal to establish generalizability. The current framing as a method paper is weakened by the negative results, but as an empirical analysis it has merit.

No fabricated citations were found - all referenced papers (StatA, SegDebias, ReTA, GS-Bias, etc.) are real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues.
